channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Justices Hint at Strains as Supreme Court Comes Under Scrutiny
item.description: Traveling across the country, justices defend the role of the court even as strained relations among its members emerge in writing and remarks.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson of the Supreme Court talks with Judge Richard Gergel of the U.S. District Court in South Carolina during a fireside chat hosted by the American Law Institute in Washington on Monday.

item.title: Trump’s New $1.8 Billion Pot of Money, and a Deadly Mosque Attack in California
item.description: Plus, a North Korean soccer team hits the road.
item.dc:creator: Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, two of his sons and his family business had argued in the case that the Internal Revenue Service should have done more to prevent a former contractor from leaking tax information.

item.title: G.O.P. Supporters Back Trump, but a Third Seek a New Direction for the Party
item.description: While President Trump remains overwhelmingly popular within the Republican coalition, a New York Times/Siena poll found, a sizable share wants the party’s next nominee to take a different approach.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Last-Minute Donation Could Upend G.O.P. Primary for Texas Attorney General
item.description: A single donor’s contribution of $2.75 million could help propel Representative Chip Roy in his clash with a self-funded state senator, Mayes Middleton.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy

item.title: The Race to Control Congress
item.description: We look at where the parties stand leading up to the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie campaigning in Kentucky last week.

item.title: A Fearful Phone Call and Frantic Search Preceded San Diego Mosque Shooting
item.description: San Diego police arrived to find three people dead. Nearby, they found two teenagers, the attackers, dead in a car. The violence shattered an idyllic Southern California city.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango, Christina Morales, Candice Reed and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers raced around San Diego before converging at the mosque.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting: What to Know About the Suspects and What Happened
item.description: Three men were killed in the attack at the Islamic Center of San Diego. Two teenage suspects were found dead in a car nearby, police said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Families escorting their children from the Islamic Center in San Diego, after a deadly shooting on Monday.

item.title: The LIRR Strike Is Over. Here’s What Commuters Can Expect on Tuesday.
item.description: A deal was reached on Monday night to end the strike that shut down America’s busiest passenger rail line, but officials said the service will not fully resume until Tuesday afternoon.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen and Qasim Nauman
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Long Island Rail Road workers went on strike on May 16 after three years of failed contract negotiations.

item.title: Trump Threatens Iran and Then Pulls Back, All in the Same Day
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly said he’ll restart military action against Iran, only to stop short of plunging the United States directly back into an unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: Middle East on Edge After Trump Says He Delayed Attack on Iran
item.description: President Trump said he had postponed a “very major attack” against Iran, as Pakistan continued its mediation efforts to end the war.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump said the U.S. military was prepared for a “full, large scale assault” against Iran.

item.title: Oil Slick Reaches a Pristine Persian Gulf Island in Iran
item.description: Videos show birds, turtles and crabs trapped inside mounds of tar around Shidvar island, a protected wildlife sanctuary with turquoise waters and white sand beaches.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Erika Solomon and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A video verified by The Times shows oil coating the shoreline of Shidvar Island as smoke rises from the Lavan refinery, seen in the distance.

item.title: Here’s How Iran Could Respond to Renewed U.S.-Israeli Strikes
item.description: In any new round of fighting, Iranian officials could adopt new tactics, including intensified strikes on neighbors and trying to close off a second strait.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iranians have used their proximity to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical trade waterway, to exert immense pressure on the global economy.

item.title: Top Treasury Lawyer Resigns After Creation of ‘Anti-Weaponization Fund’
item.description: Brian Morrissey, the department’s general counsel, stepped down hours after the Trump administration announced the $1.8 billion fund.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Treasury Department’s general counsel, Brian Morrissey, resigned on Monday, three people familiar with the move said.

item.title: Judge Bars ICE From Making Immigration Arrests at Courts in New York
item.description: The Trump administration’s practice of detaining migrants at federal courthouses had stirred outrage.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mask-wearing federal agents had become a regular presence in the hallways of 26 Federal Plaza in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: Trump’s Deportations Are Costing Americans Jobs
item.description: According to a new study, construction was impacted more than any other industry studied, with American-born workers losing more jobs than immigrants as a result of the deportations.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The home building industry, already strained, faces a new challenge amid a surge in deportations.

item.title: Trump Moves to Admit 10,000 More White South Africans as Refugees
item.description: The Trump administration argues that an “emergency refugee situation” in South Africa merits bringing more Afrikaners to America, at a cost of some $100 million.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: President Trump and his aides have claimed that white South Africans face racial persecution, an assertion strongly disputed by officials in that country.

item.title: ICE Agent Charged in Shooting of a Venezuelan Immigrant in Minnesota
item.description: The agent is the second federal officer to face felony charges in Minnesota stemming from Operation Metro Surge, the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police blocked off an area in Minneapolis near the shooting on Jan. 14.

item.title: W.H.O. Chief Is ‘Deeply Concerned’ by Speed and Scale of Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Health officials reported more than 130 suspected deaths and 513 cases, a sharp rise since the outbreak was first identified in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Lynsey Chutel
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A visitor has their temperature checked before entering Kyeshero Hospital in Goma on Monday.

item.title: American Man Tests Positive for Ebola Amid Outbreak, Officials Say
item.description: An American doctor working with a Christian missionary organization in the Democratic Republic of Congo was exposed to the deadly virus while treating patients, the organization said.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Hannah Ziegler
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: People wait near an ambulance at a hospital in Bunia, Congo on Sunday.

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak as Cases and Deaths Rise
item.description: More than 130 deaths and over 500 cases are suspected. The United States restricted entry for people who have been to three African countries after a global health emergency was declared.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Outside a hospital in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Sunday.

item.title: As Ukraine Strikes Russian Oil Sites, an Environmental Disaster Unfolds
item.description: After invading more than four years ago, Moscow has usually been the one causing ecological disaster. But Kyiv’s strikes, intended to cut into the Kremlin’s oil revenue, have flipped the script.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva and Alina Lobzina
item.media:description: A satellite image from the aerial mapping company Vantor of a Russian oil facility in Tuapse, Russia, after Ukrainian drone attacks in April.

item.title: Sandy Fire in Simi Valley Threatens Thousands of Homes
item.description: Officials ordered more than 33,000 people to evacuate after a brush fire broke out in Simi Valley, Calif. Fire activity had slowed by Monday night, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Amy Graff
item.media:description: The blaze, called the Sandy fire, broke out on a warm, breezy day in Simi Valley.

item.title: On Blind, Anxious Tech Workers Get the Lowdown on Layoffs
item.description: On the website Blind, professionals share advice — and gallows humor — anonymously. It is chronicling the curdling of tech optimism.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sunguk Moon, a founder and the chief executive of Blind, said sentiment on the site “went from personal career planning to mass anxiety.”

item.title: As OpenAI Celebrates Court Win Against Musk, Other Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: A jury’s rejection of Elon Musk’s $150 billion lawsuit against OpenAI was a major hurdle crossed. But the maker of ChatGPT faces a list of other problems.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Natallie Rocha
item.media:description: Lawyers for OpenAI, who won a resounding victory on Monday, outside the federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif.

item.title: Silicon Valley’s Answer to Declining Male Fertility? Sperm Racing.
item.description: A San Francisco biotech start-up races sex cells on tiny tracks. Can an internet joke become a serious business?
item.dc:creator: Daniel Waite Penny

item.title: Is There a Door No. 3 for Democrats?
item.description: Is there anything Democrats can do to break free of a deeply polarized political system in which parties are constantly winning and then losing office?
item.dc:creator: Thomas B. Edsall
item.media:credit: Joshua Dudley Greer for The New York Times

item.title: Military Bases Are Rife With ‘Forever Chemicals.’ New Mexico Wants Them Cleaned Up.
item.description: The state is leading the country’s reckoning with PFAS. The outcome of its suit against the federal government will affect how courts treat more than 15,000 other claims nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan and Nina Riggio
item.media:description: Art Schaap, right, a dairy farmer in Clovis, N.M., and James Kenney, of the New Mexico Environment Department, stand in the area where Mr. Schaap had to kill his cattle.

item.title: Calls for ‘No Seed Oil’ Push Companies to Order Up Butter and Beef Tallow
item.description: Businesses are finding different (and more costly) ways to fry foods as shoppers demand alternatives to seed oils as part of the Make America Healthy Again movement.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell

item.title: Interest in N.Y.C. Pre-K Programs Is Flat, Despite Mamdani’s Outreach
item.description: Expanding child care is a pillar of Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s affordability agenda. Newly released application numbers may raise questions about strategy and demand.
item.dc:creator: Eliza Shapiro and Matthew Haag
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani got budget help from Gov. Kathy Hochul this year to fund pre-K expansion.

item.title: Dallas World Cup Mural: Artist Wyland Says Beloved Whale Artwork Was Painted Over
item.description: The artwork’s removal ahead of the World Cup has ignited a backlash over public art and what cities are willing to sacrifice for global events.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:description: The mural “Ocean Life” in downtown Dallas that is being painted over in preparation for the World Cup was dedicated in 1999.
